title: Molecular Docking - An easy protocol
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"1v4mf","text":"To setup the docking, the Lamarckian genetic algorithm (LGA) was used, and docked conformations were compared and clustered based on the conformational similarities and root-mean-square positional deviation (rmsd).  The numbers of runs were adjusted to 30 for all ligands. Rmsd was calculated based on rmsd conformational clustering tolerance of 2.0 Å. For genetic algorithms, parameters were setup as 150, 0.02, 0.8, 27000, and 2500000 for population size, mutation rate, crossover rate, maximum number of generation, and maximum number of energy evaluations, respectively. For LGA docking, the pseudo-Solis and Wets local search method was used with a maximum of 300 iterations per local search, and the probability of performing local search on an individual in the population was adjusted to 0.06, the size of local search space to sample, ρ, was 1.0, the maximum number of consecutive successes or failures before doubling or halving the ρ was four for both, and finally the lower bound on ρ was 0.01. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b7adb","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8cfnp","text":"To have access to sample data and configuration files also check out: doi: 10.13140/RG.2.2.20407.42403","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":75,"length":27}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"3035B383AB4D49D086556C412C1325BF","url":"https://doi.org/10.13140/RG.2.2.20407.42403"}}}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"a48i9","text":"For each target, you need a separate folder in which you have folders of compounds (ligands). For each ligand, separate docking algorithm should be run.","type":"align-justify","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"19blt","text":"Install all required software:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"45dou","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"69jr8","text":"MGL tools 4.2","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e9nf4","text":"UCSF Chimera","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2asdc","text":" Accelrys Discovery Studio","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"erusn","text":"Open babel","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b5tv1","text":"Cygwin","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7otlf","text":"ChemSketch","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"enqjv","text":"SPDV Swiss PDBVIEWER","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Each compound should have pdb extension. So using Open babel you need to convert the files from mol, sdf, etc to pdb.
Download your target from protein data bank . For example: 4jkv Search pdb for 4jkv, and then download 4jkv.
Open protein pdb file.

Delet all HETATM lines we are refered to the previous dockied ligand. The last line should be END. Edeit all HETAM till you have END after the last ATOM (Which refered the last atom of the last residue). If water is important for your docking you can have HETATM related to HOH.
Open SPDBV
Open your protein.pdb (edited version).
Using the top menu select All.
Using Prefs at the top menu, click on Energy Minimization, and click OK.
Save
Open protein.pdb in notpad++
Delet all coordinates related to SPDBV till END.
Save
Copy the last protein pdb file into all folders of your compounds.
Open AutoDock 4.2
From the top menu (Read Molecule), go to the first folder and choose your protein to open it in AutoDock.
EDIT/HYDROGEN/ADD/PPLAR ONLY Click OK.
EDIT/CHARGES/ADD KOLLMAN CHARGES
EDIT/ATOMS/ASSIGN AD4 TYPE

SAVE/WRITE PDBQT BROWSE and go to your first folder of which you already read the molecule. Save the pdbqt file so that the file name is as the same as your pdb file. Now select from ATOM to END and ADD Check box for SORT NODES Check box for SAVE TRANSFORMING COODS OK If your pdb file is 4jkv.pdb , your pdbqt file should be 4jkv.pdbqt. Later on, carefully save your files in your previous folder (project). If you mistakenly save some files in other folders, you will definitely get error during your run.
Using AutoDock and the LIGAND menu (INPUT), open the first LIGAND . Open 1.pdb file. You can have numbers for your ligand folders. 1.pdb is located in your first folder.
LIGAND/TORSION TREE/DETECT ROOT
LIGAND/TORSION TREE/CHOOSE ROOT
LIGAND/OUTPUT/SAVE PDBQT Beside protein.pdbqt, now you have 1.pdbqt in the same folder. Again before saving, check the folder at which you are saving.
GRID/MACROMOLECULES/CHOOSE Select your protein (e.g. 4jkv). SELECT MOLECULE OK Go to your folder and save it as your protein.pdbqt. SAVE YES (Replace the file)
GRID/SET MAP TYPES/CHOOSE LIGAND Click on 1. SELECT LIGAND

GRID/GRID BOX/CENTER/Picj=k an atom and Center Grid Box: You must set the center of grid box on which the ligand is docked. X= Y= Z= You must already determine where you want to dock and identify the docking site or active site of your target. For example: For 4jkv, these are suggested: X= -20.101 Y= 21.355 Z= 28.384 If you want to perform blind docking to randomly dock, your grid box should be large enough to cover the whole protein.
CLOSE SAVING CURRENT
GRID/OUTPUT/SAVE GPF Go to your folder (for example the folder number 1) and save protein.gpf . your grid parameter file (GPF) shoud be as the same as your protein (e.g. 4jkv.gpf).
DOCKING/MACROMOLECULES/SET REGID FILE NAME Go to your folder. Click on protein.pdbqt. OPEN
DOCKING/LIGAND/CHOOSE Click on 1 (your ligand) SELECT LIGAND ACCEPT the default parameters.
DOCKING/SEARCH PARAMETERS/GENETIC ALGORITHM ACCEPT the default parameters.
DOCKING/OUTPUT/LAMARCKIAN GA Go to your folder SAVE protein.dpf
SETUP FOR THE FIRST COMPOUND AND TARGET IS FINISHED, YOU CAN CLOSE AUTODOCK OR REMOVE ALL OPENED MOLECULES TO START THE SECOND SETUP.
OPEN CYGWIN TERMINAL

You must go to your folder. cd e:/cd proteincd 1 Linux Cygwin cd e:/ cd protein cd 1 Linux Cygwin you must already have two exe files in your folders. Copy autogrid4.exe and autodock4.exe to alll ligand folders. To have these files, go the the link located at the end of abstract of this protocol. A sample data contain one studied docking project in which you can find exe files as well. you must already have two exe files in your folders. Copy autogrid4.exe and autodock4.exe to alll ligand folders. To have these files, go the the link located at the end of abstract of this protocol. A sample data contain one studied docking project in which you can find exe files as well.
$ ./autogrid4.exe –p target.gpf –l target.glg &$ tail –f target.glg Linux Cygwin $ ./autogrid4.exe –p target.gpf –l target.glg & $ tail –f target.glg Linux Cygwin
$ ./autodock4.exe –p target.dpf –l target.dlg &$ tail –f target.dlg Linux Cygwin $ ./autodock4.exe –p target.dpf –l target.dlg & $ tail –f target.dlg Linux Cygwin

$ grep '^DOCKED' target.dlg | cut -c9->target_run.pdbqt$ cut -c-66 target_run.pdbqt>target_run.pdb Linux Cygwin $ grep '^DOCKED' target.dlg | cut -c9->target_run.pdbqt $ cut -c-66 target_run.pdbqt>target_run.pdb Linux Cygwin
You can see the binding energy calculations and inhibition constants.
Now you can open your docked files (target_run.pdb) by UCSF Chimera and Accelrys DS , and analyze the interaction between protein and ligand. You can also use LigPlus .

Please use and cite the following paper: Kaboli, P. J.; Bazrafkan, M.; Ismail, P.; Ling, K. H. Molecular Modelling of Berberine Derivatives as Inhibitors of Human Smoothened Receptor and Hedgehog Signalling Pathway Using a Newly Developed Algorithm on Anti-Cancer Drugs. Recent Pat. Anticancer. Drug Discov. 2017 , 12 (4), 384–400.
creator: Parham Jabbarzadeh Kaboli
